title: Apply: Sixth Amendment
description: Apply your understanding of the Sixth Amendment with this set of multiple-choice questions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Declaration and Resolves of the First Continental Congress (1774).**

The good people of the several colonies of...justly alarmed at these [[☃ definition 1]] proceedings of parliament and administration, have [[☃ definition 2]] elected, constituted, and appointed deputies to meet, and sit in general Congress...in order to obtain such establishment, as that their religion, laws, and liberties, may not be [[☃ definition 3]]: Whereupon the deputies so appointed being now assembled...as Englishmen, their ancestors in like cases have usually done, for asserting and [[☃ definition 4]] their rights and liberties, DECLARE...

***Resolved,*** N.C.D. 5. That the respective colonies are entitled to the common law of England, and more especially to the great and inestimable privilege of being tried by their peers of the [[☃ definition 5]], according to the course of that law. 

**Which protection, eventually written into the Sixth Amendment, did the colonists call for in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: characterized by unrestrained and tyrannical authority
the area near or surrounding a place

choices.content: The right to a speedy trial
The right to trial by jury
The right to a lawyer
The right to confront witnesses

assessment_items.question_content:
Laura is charged with felony theft. Because she cannot afford an attorney, a public defender is appointed to her case. The public defender is overwhelmed with cases and does not have time to review Laura's case before it goes to trial. During the trial, the public defender does not present any evidence in Laura’s defense or call any witnesses to testify on her behalf. He also makes several errors in procedural matters. The jury convicts Laura of felony theft. She later learns that her attorney’s lack of preparation and inadequate representation significantly impacted the outcome of her trial.

**This scenario relates to which protection in the Sixth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to a speedy trial
The right to a jury trial
The right to a lawyer
The right to know legal charges

assessment_items.question_content:
James is arrested on a minor drug possession charge. Due to an administrative backlog and repeated delays, James’s trial was postponed multiple times over a period of two years. Despite his attorney’s requests for the trial to begin, the trial continues to be delayed. James faces prolonged stress and uncertainty, which ultimately affects his job and personal life.

**This scenario relates to which protection in the Sixth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right against self-incrimination
The right to summon witnesses
The right to confront witnesses
The right to a speedy trial

assessment_items.question_content:
Maria is charged with assault in a small town where she is well-known. During jury selection, the defense raises concerns about the potential for bias due to the high-profile nature of the case and Maria’s local reputation. Despite these concerns, the court allows the trial to proceed with jurors who are familiar with Maria and her case from local news coverage. 

**This scenario touches on which protection in the Sixth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to an impartial jury
The right to a lawyer
The right against double jeopardy
The right to due process

assessment_items.question_content:
Haruki is on trial for a serious robbery charge. The judge decides to close the courtroom to everyone besides people involved in the trial,  such as lawyers, the jury, and the defendant. Haruki’s family and other members of his community are unable to attend. Reporters are also prevented from attending the trial. 

**This scenario touches on which protection in the Sixth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The right to a speedy trial
The right to a public trial
The right against self-incrimination
The right to an impartial jury

assessment_items.question_content:
During Amina’s trial for [[☃ definition 1]], the prosecution introduces a statement from a key witness who is unable to appear in court due because he is out of the country. The witness’s statement is read aloud to the jury, but Amina’s defense attorney is not allowed to cross-examine the witness or challenge their statement. 

**This scenario relates to which protection in the Sixth Amendment?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: the crime of stealing money that belongs to an organization or employer you work for

choices.content: The right to confront witnesses
The right to due process
The right to a jury trial
The right to an impartial jury

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the majority opinion in *Gideon v. Wainwright* (1963).**

Governments, both state and federal, quite properly spend vast sums of money to establish machinery to try defendants accused of crime. Lawyers to [[☃ definition 1]] are everywhere [[☃ definition 2]] essential to protect the public's interest in an orderly society. Similarly, there are few defendants charged with crime, few indeed, who fail to hire the best lawyers they can get to prepare and present their defenses. That [the] government hires lawyers to prosecute and defendants who have the money hire lawyers to defend are the strongest indications of the widespread belief that lawyers in criminal courts are necessities, not luxuries. The right of one charged with crime to counsel may not be deemed fundamental and essential to fair trials in some countries, but it is in ours.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: prove in court that someone is guilty of a crime

choices.content: Federal and state governments already spend so much money on trials that it would be unfair to require the government to provide legal counsel to defendants who can’t afford attorneys.
Lawyers play an essential role in ensuring that trials are fair, which is evident by how much money governments and people spend on them.
Lawyers can make an important contribution to trials, but this  does not necessarily mean that people need lawyers to have fair trials.
Other countries do not require their governments to provide lawyers to people too poor to afford legal fees, and the United States needs to follow their example.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from William Blackstone’s *Commentaries on the Laws of England* (1769).**

Our law has therefore wisely placed this strong and two-fold barrier, of a [[☃ definition 1]] and a trial by jury, between the liberties of the people, and the [[☃ definition 2]]. . . . [T]he founders of the English law have, with excellent forecast, [[☃ definition 3]], that . . . the truth of every accusation, whether preferred in the shape of [[☃ definition 4]], information, or appeal, should afterwards be confirmed by the unanimous [[☃ definition 5]] of twelve of his equals and neighbours, [[☃ definition 6]] chosen and superior to all suspicion.

**Which statement best summarizes Blackstone’s argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: an official, written accusation of a crime
a right or power that only a monarch has

choices.content: Court cases do not need jury trials as laws are written perfectly and ensure that any violations have clear and reasonable punishments.
The right to a trial by jury is not crucial for a functioning democracy, as long as judges are moral.
The use of jury trials protects people accused of crimes from being unfairly treated by the government.
Juries are only necessary in monarchies, as only kings can select people who have the morals necessary to rule on court cases.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from the Supreme Court’s opinion in *Powell v. Alabama* (1932). In this case, nine young Black men were accused of assaulting two White women. The men’s three trials took one day and all were sentenced to death. The Supreme Court was asked to rule on whether Alabama violated the Due Process Clause of the Fourteenth Amendment by rushing the cases.**

[T]he right to be heard would be, in many cases, of little [[☃ definition 1]] if it did not [[☃ definition 7]] the right to be heard by [[☃ definition 2]]. Even the intelligent and educated [[☃ definition 3]] has small and sometimes no skill in the science of law. If charged with a crime, he is incapable, generally, of determining for himself whether the [[☃ definition 4]] is good or bad. He is unfamiliar with the rules of evidence. Left without the aid of counsel, he may be put to trial without a proper charge, and convicted upon [[☃ definition 5]] evidence, or evidence irrelevant to the issue or otherwise [[☃ definition 6]]. . . . He requires the hand of counsel at every step of the proceedings against him. . . . If that be true of men of intelligence, how much more true is it of the ignorant or illiterate, or those of feeble intellect.

**Which statement best summarizes the argument in the excerpt?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.definition: a person without professional or specialized knowledge
a formal accusation of a crime
not allowed to be submitted as evidence in a trial

choices.content: The state can encourage people to have a lawyer, but it is not responsible for providing a lawyer for those that need one.
The state must provide a lawyer for all people, even if they want to defend themselves, because a trial is invalid without a defense lawyer.
Most people need the advice of a lawyer in criminal cases, as they do not understand all of the rules and complexities of trials.
Most people could reasonably defend themselves in a criminal court case, as the laws are generally easy to understand.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from an article printed in Philadelphia’s *Independent Gazetteer* in 1787. The author uses the [[☃ definition 1]] An Old Whig.**

The author of the speech tells us, that a bill of rights would have been [[☃ definition 2]] and absurd; because “no powers are given to Congress but what are expressly given;” and “that we shall still enjoy those privileges of which we are not [[☃ definition 3]] either by the intention or the act that brought that body into existence.” . . . 

Any future Congress sitting under the authority of the proposed new constitution, may, if they choose, enact that there shall be no more trial by jury, in any of the United States; except in the trial of crimes; and this “supreme law” will at once [[☃ definition 4]] the trial by jury, in all other cases. . . . 

At this rate Judges may sit in the United States, as they did in some instances before the war, without a jury to condemn people’s property and extract money from their pockets, to be put into the pockets of the judges themselves who condemn them; and we shall be told that we are safe from the oppression of government.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Congress is appropriately restricted from passing laws that affect the judicial branch because of the separation of powers doctrine.
The new Constitution gives too much power to the judges in the federal judiciary because they cannot be removed from office for immoral behavior.
An amendment to cover the right to trial by jury is unnecessary because each state confirms the right to trial by jury in their state constitutions.
Without an amendment to protect the right to trial by jury, nothing is to stop judges from violating people’s right to a fair trial.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The North-American Review and Miscellaneous Journal* (1820).**

It is, doubtless, a very common opinion that the confession of the party accused is the highest species of proof. ‘Out of thine own mouth will I judge thee,’ is the process which most men prefer to all others. . . . But this is far from being the light in which confessions ought to be regarded, by the [[☃ definition 1]] for the prosecution of crimes. A judicial confession, made and persisted in understandingly and [[☃ definition 2]] before a competent judge, is indeed conclusive. But an [[☃ definition 3]] and voluntary [[☃ definition 4]] of guilt is ever to be received with great caution, and is entirely open to contradiction or explanation. . . . It must be the voluntary act of the prisoner, unmoved by promises or threats; and great allowance is always made for the state of mind produced by the situation in which he is placed.

**Which statement best summarizes the author’s argument?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: People are not educated enough to represent themselves in a courtroom and, for a trial to be fair, they must have the advice of legal counsel.
It is very unlikely that someone would falsely confess to a crime, so long as the punishments for crimes are appropriately harsh.
The courts should only accept a guilty plea if the person confessing is doing so of their own free will and not out of fear or to try to get a lesser punishment.
The person accused of committing a crime is responsible for providing evidence of their innocence, as defendants are assumed to be guilty until proven innocent.

assessment_items.question_content:
**This is an excerpt from *The North-American Review and Miscellaneous Journal* (1820).**

It is, doubtless, a very common opinion that the confession of the party accused is the highest species of proof. ‘Out of thine own mouth will I judge thee,’ is the process which most men prefer to all others. . . . But this is far from being the light in which confessions ought to be regarded, by the [[☃ definition 1]] for the prosecution of crimes. A judicial confession, made and persisted in understandingly and [[☃ definition 2]] before a competent judge, is indeed conclusive. But an [[☃ definition 3]] and voluntary [[☃ definition 4]] of guilt is ever to be received with great caution, and is entirely open to contradiction or explanation. . . . It must be the voluntary act of the prisoner, unmoved by promises or threats; and great allowance is always made for the state of mind produced by the situation in which he is placed.

**According to the excerpt, the author most likely opposes which of the following?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The use of plea bargaining
The right to legal counsel
The use of juries in trials
The ability to summon witnesses
